text: The video displays an external view of the surgical setup for a right lower lobectomy, featuring a subxiphoid port, an anterior camera port, and two anterior utility ports. The Artessential VATS wristed instruments are shown fitting through eight-millimeter robotic ports or being inserted directly through the chest wall.

The surgeon uses a bipolar Maryland dissector for adhesiolysis, demonstrating the ease of movement and bilateral hand usage. The instrument is angled precisely with wrist movements, and the bipolar energy device is employed to coagulate small vessels, minimizing energy displacement.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to address an incomplete fissure, initially taking the inferior pulmonary vein. The instruments are switched between a sucker and a standard diathermy, demonstrating ease and speed of use as the surgeon operates directly from the patient's bedside.

The surgeon performs dissection around the anterior hilum using a bipolar Maryland dissector, precisely following the tissue planes. Bipolar fenestrated forceps are used for blunt dissection, resembling the precision of a robotic procedure.

The surgeon uses bipolar fenestrated forceps to dissect around the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing the path for stapling. The wristed instruments allow the surgeon to perform stapling independently, without relying on an assistant.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to carefully dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, identifying the superior pulmonary vein and pulmonary artery. Fenestrated forceps are then employed to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, demonstrating precise articulated movement with a ninety-degree angle. The blunt tip of the forceps allows safe dissection around large blood vessels. The surgeon performs stapling at the bedside using the subxiphoid port for lobectomies.

The surgeon identifies lymph nodes located behind the superior pulmonary vein and uses fenestrated forceps to grasp them at various angles without causing damage. The bipolar Maryland dissector is then employed to gently dissect the lymph nodes out, showcasing the maneuverability of wristed instruments in a VATS procedure.

The surgeon dissects around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch using alternating Maryland dissectors and fenestrated forceps. The instruments' angulation allows precise manipulation around the vessel, creating a clear path for subsequent stapling.

The surgeon uses the Cavidian Tristapler with the Signia gun introduced through the subxiphoid port to fire staples. During the thymectomy, dissection begins at the junction of the pericardium and fat, following the phrenic nerve upwards. The bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps are used for retraction and dissection, providing excellent angles and subtle wrist movements. The innominate vein, rhema vein, and superior vena cava (SVC) are visible, with fat being dissected off between the innominate and rhema veins using sweeping motions.

The surgeon performs a thymectomy using a left-sided approach due to the presence of a left superior vena cava (SVC). The tumor is visible above the surgical instruments, with the phrenic nerve lying over the SVC. Wristed Maryland dissectors and fenestrated forceps are used to navigate the tissue planes effectively, demonstrating their suitability for the angles required in this case.
